title: fix(homepage): render widgets progressively and batch permission checks
body:
### What does it do?

- Refactors homepage widget loading to remove page-wide blocking during permission resolution.
- Adds per-widget permission state (`loading` / `allowed` / `denied`) so the homepage grid renders immediately and each widget resolves independently.
- Starts expensive homepage data queries (`count-documents`, `key-statistics`) as soon as the homepage route mounts, in parallel with layout and permission resolution.
- Adds a new v5 large-data seed script for `examples/complex` (`seed:v5`) with configurable volume (`--multiplier` / `SEED_MULTIPLIER`) to support perf validation.

### Why is it needed?

- On large datasets, homepage performance degrades because widget content (notably document counts) is heavy.
- Previously, the homepage stayed in a global loading state until all widget permission checks completed, delaying first meaningful render.
- Users had to remove widgets to improve perceived performance; this change improves responsiveness without requiring layout changes

### How to test it?

- Environment:
  - Run from `examples/complex`
  - Use PostgreSQL or MySQL local DB via existing scripts (`develop:postgres` / `develop:mysql`)
- Seed large data:
  - `yarn seed:v5 -- --multiplier 100`
- Verify homepage behavior (admin app):
  - Open `/admin` homepage
  - Confirm grid renders immediately (no full-page blocking spinner while permissions resolve)
  - Confirm widgets show their own loading states and appear progressively
  - Confirm no permission regression (denied widgets remain hidden/unavailable)

title: enhancement(content-manager): add documentid in listview and editview
body: ### What does it do? In the List view: * Display a new "documentId" column * Remove the "id" column * Add a Copy icon to allow copying to clipboard In the Edit view: * Display a new "Document ID" at the bottom of the Actions menu if the document has been created (in at least one locale when i18n is enabled) and the content-type is a collection-type (_disabled for single-types as there's no need in the API to know the document ID of a single-type_) * Add a new "Copy the document ID" in the Actions menu to allow copying to clipboard ### Why is it needed? Having the "id" column brought confusion to users as it is now the document ID that's used to fetch data from the API. ### How to test it? **By default, in the list view, the column "Document ID" might not be visible, you may have to display it through the Configure the view** _Single types_ * Go to a single type * Create an entry (fill in one field and save) * Click on the "More actions" button on the top right corner => it should stay as before, ie.: _Collection types - New entry_ * Go to a collection type (with i18n enabled) * Create an entry (do not fill in a field and save yet) * Click on the "More actions" button on the top right corner => the Document ID shouldn't be visible in this menu as the entry hasn't yet been created, so the menu should look like this: _Collection types - Existing entry_ * Create that entry (fill in one field and save) * Click on the "More actions" button on the top right corner => Now the menu should show the "Copy document ID" button + the "Document ID" value at the bottom like so: * Click on the "Copy document ID" => there should be a success notification and ID should be copied to your clipboard * Go back to the List view => there should be a new column "DocumentID" * Click on the Copy icon => there should be a success notification and ID should be copied to your clipboard _Collection types - New locale (from existing entry)_ * Click on your entry * Select "Create ... locale" to create an entry in a new locale => The menu should still show the document ID and the "Copy document ID" button since it exists in at least one locale ### Related issue(s)/PR(s) Fixes https://github.com/strapi/strapi/issues/21967 Fixes CMS-497 according to product needs https://www.loom.com/share/a9b87246cff84f5cb906a3725bf75e7b?sid=111510e4-b64a-40e0-b13c-6de2587becb1

title: future(upload): add upload progress dialog
body:
### What does it do?
- Adds a new upload progress dialog to the Media Library (future upload UI) so users can see upload progress/status during file uploads.
- Introduces upload progress state management (new future store + hooks) and wires it through the upload admin api layer.
- Improves robustness by handling “catastrophic” upload errors (i.e. failures that weren’t being surfaced/handled cleanly).
- Adds retry support for cancelled uploads.
- Adds streaming endpoint

### Why is it needed?

So the user has feedback on their upload while it is in progress not matter where they are in the app

### How to test it?

Go to the unstable media libraray (add to .env UNSTABLE_MEDIA_LIBRARY=true)
Click some files to upload, you should see their progress in a dialog in the bottom right corner

In `plugins.js` add a config like this
```
upload: {
    config: {
      security: {
        allowedTypes: ['image/*'],
      },
    },
  },
  ```
Upload some images and any other file type
The images should upload successfully and errors should be reported for the other file types

Upload any file type but images
The error should be reported in the dialog

Upload a bunch of files then click cancel
You should see files that uploaded and the files that were cancelled
Click retry and the cancelled files should resume uploading (note this differs from the design which says cancel all but I think that's impossible or we would have to delete the files uploaded)

**Design Note**
I validated with Anka that the upload progress bar can be represented as indeterminate




https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1d309519-0cd9-46a2-a94e-2478f8715567

title: chore(scripts/check-package-versions): support Yarn catalog
body: ### What does it do? * Updates `.syncpackrc.json` to explicitly define a version group for Yarn `catalog:` specifiers using `specifierTypes: ["unsupported"]`. * Adds a defensive guard for `catalog:` values in `scripts/check-package-versions.mjs`. * Moves `eslint` from `dependencies` to `devDependencies` in `examples/plugins/todo-example/package.json`. ### Why is it needed? This PR addresses a regression in the `check-package-versions` GitHub workflow. The workflow was failing due to an `eslint` version mismatch in `examples/plugins/todo-example/package.json`, not because of `catalog:` specifiers as initially suspected. The changes: * Ensure `catalog:` specifiers are explicitly handled by `syncpack` for clarity and future compatibility (e.g., with `syncpack` v14). * Correct `eslint`'s dependency type in the example package, preventing future spurious `HighestSemverMismatch` failures. ### How to test it? 1. Run `yarn install` 2. Run `yarn version:check` 3. Verify that no errors are reported and the output shows `catalog:` entries handled in their own group. 4. Verify that CI checks pass. ### Related issue(s)/PR(s) * Related to the introduction of Yarn catalog feature in #25400. ---
author: nclsndr

reviews.body: ## Pull request overview This PR updates the repo’s package-version consistency tooling to explicitly account for Yarn `catalog:` dependency specifiers (via `syncpack`) and fixes a dependency classification issue that was breaking the `check-package-versions` workflow. **Changes:** - Adds an ignored Syncpack version group for Yarn `catalog:` references (classified as `unsupported` specifiers by Syncpack v13). - Adds a defensive `catalog:` guard in `scripts/check-package-versions.mjs` to avoid treating `catalog:` as a semver version. - Moves `eslint` from `dependencies` to `devDependencies` in the todo example plugin package. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 3 out of 3 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `scripts/check-package-versions.mjs` | Adds defensive handling and documentation around Yarn `catalog:` values to avoid semver enforcement. | | `examples/plugins/todo-example/package.json` | Fixes dependency type for `eslint` to prevent version-check mismatches. | | `.syncpackrc.json` | Adds an explicit (ignored) version group to classify/handle Yarn `catalog:` specifiers via `unsupported` specifier type. | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
There's a merge conflict, but otherwise LGTM

commits.message:
fix(ci): explicit catalog: handling in version check scripts

- .syncpackrc.json: add a dedicated 'Yarn catalog references' version
  group (specifierTypes: unsupported) before the generic !exact group.
  syncpack v13 has no native catalog: support, so npa throws on that
  specifier and syncpack falls through to UnsupportedSpecifier. The new
  group makes the intent explicit and will survive a syncpack upgrade.
- check-package-versions.mjs: add a defensive catalog: guard in
  shouldEnforceVersion plus a comment explaining why catalog: entries in
  dependency fields are invisible to this script.
- examples/plugins/todo-example/package.json: move eslint from
  dependencies to devDependencies (it is a lint tool, not a runtime dep)
  to avoid spurious HighestSemverMismatch failures when other packages
  bump the eslint dev-dep version.

title: fix: preserve bidirectional order
body:
## What does it do?

This change ensures that bidirectional many-to-many (M2M) relation ordering is preserved on **both sides** of the join table during publish/unpublish operations.

## Why is it needed?

When a bidirectional ordered M2M relation is published in Strapi, the system deletes and recreates join rows for the published entry, which breaks the original order on the **inverse side** (e.g., all related entries become "last" in order).

A second issue arises with "interleaved" publishing: if some related entries (e.g., articles) are published before the owner (author), the author's article order diverges between draft and published states because only the inverse side ordering was captured and restored.

## How to test

Comprehensive automated API tests exist. Run:

```
yarn test:api -- --testPathPattern="bidirectional-relations"
```

**Test 1 — Inverse Side (unpublish → republish):**
1. Create an Article with Authors A, B, C in a specific order.
2. Publish all, then unpublish Author B.
3. Republish Author B.
4. Verify Article → Authors order matches the original and is _not_ reset to "last".



## Related issue(s)/PR(s)

CMS-166
author: jhoward1994

title: test(api): add querylimit middleware test
body:
### What does it do?

Adds an API integration test (`tests/api/core/strapi/api/populate/query-array-limit-populate.test.api.js`) that:

- Builds a content type with 105 distinct one-to-one relations and requests `populate[0]`…`populate[104]` in the query string (more than the default `qs` `arrayLimit` of 100).
- **With default `strapi::query`:** asserts the request does not return `200` (broken/mis-parsed populate when indexed keys exceed the limit).
- **With `strapi::query` configured with a higher `arrayLimit` (via `register` in the test app):** asserts `200` and that populated data is actually returned: linked relation `r0` includes the expected leaf `name`, and an unlinked relation `r1` is `null`.

This will serve as evidence we officially support this method, and ensure we don't break it in the future.

### Why is it needed?

More than 100 indexed `populate[*]` query parameters are parsed by `qs` as an object instead of an array, which breaks populate handling. Raising `arrayLimit` on `strapi::query` is the supported approach (see discussion on [#25633](https://github.com/strapi/strapi/pull/25633)). This test locks in that behavior and documents it in code so regressions are caught.

### How to test it?

```bash
yarn test:api query-array-limit-populate.test.api.js
```


### Related issue(s)/PR(s)

- Related: [#25632](https://github.com/strapi/strapi/issues/25632), [#25633](https://github.com/strapi/strapi/pull/25633)

- use transactions in attaching relations (entity-repository)
- adds max batch limit directly in each db dialect (dialect default and sqlite)
- use the max batch limit instead of hardcoded values and conditional checks throughout the codebase

body:
Bumps [effect](https://github.com/Effect-TS/effect/tree/HEAD/packages/effect) from 3.19.19 to 3.21.0.
Release notes

Sourced from effect's releases.

effect@3.21.0

Minor Changes

  • #5780 f7bb09b Thanks @​kitlangton! - Add Cron.prev and reverse iteration support, aligning next/prev lookup tables, fixing DST handling symmetry, and expanding cron backward/forward test coverage.

  • #5780 bd7552a Thanks @​mattiamanzati! - Add type-level utils to asserting layer types

  • #5780 ad1a7eb Thanks @​schickling! - RcMap: support dynamic idleTimeToLive values per key

    The idleTimeToLive option can now be a function that receives the key and returns a duration, allowing different TTL values for different resources.

    const map =
  yield *
  RcMap.make({
    lookup: (key: string) => acquireResource(key),
    idleTimeToLive: (key: string) => {
      if (key.startsWith("premium:")) return Duration.minutes(10)
      return Duration.minutes(1)
    }
  })

  • #5780 0d32048 Thanks @​mikearnaldi! - Fix annotateCurrentSpan, add Effect.currentPropagatedSpan

Patch Changes

  • #5780 0d32048 Thanks @​mikearnaldi! - Add logs to first propagated span, in the following case before this fix the log would not be added to the p span because Effect.fn adds a fake span for the purpose of adding a stack frame.

    import { Effect } from "effect"

    const f = Effect.fn(function* () {
    
yield* Effect.logWarning("FooBar")
    
return yield* Effect.fail("Oops")
    
})
    

    const p = f().pipe(Effect.withSpan("p"))

commits.message:
fix(upload): allow removal of file type filter in media library

The FilterList component's handleClick function only handled string filter
values. For the 'file' type filter, which uses an object structure like
{ $not: { $contains: ['image', 'video'] } }, the function would return true
(keeping the filter) instead of properly comparing and removing it.

This fix adds handling for object filter values using JSON.stringify comparison,
allowing users to properly remove the file type filter by clicking the X button.

Fixes #24737

title: fix(document-service): re-insert cascade-deleted bidirectional relations
body:
<!--
Hello 👋 Thank you for submitting a pull request.

To help us merge your PR, make sure to follow the instructions below:

- Create or update the tests
- Create or update the documentation at https://github.com/strapi/documentation
- Refer to the issue you are closing in the PR description: Fix #issue
- Specify if the PR is ready to be merged or work in progress (by opening a draft PR)

Please ensure you read the Contributing Guide: https://github.com/strapi/strapi/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md
-->
## What does it do?
Fixes bidirectionalRelations.sync() in the document service to re-insert join table entries that were removed by FK cascade delete during publish/discard-draft operations.

The fix uses an explicit existence check (SELECT … FIRST) before deciding to UPDATE (row exists, fix order) or INSERT (row was cascade-deleted, restore it). A plain UPDATE affected-row count was avoided because MySQL/MariaDB returns 0 for no-op updates (value unchanged), which would have caused spurious duplicate row inserts.

## Why is it needed?

When editors republish a related entry that another content type or component references via a bidirectional relation, the API response for the referencing entry returns stale or missing data until the Page is manually unpublished and republished.


## How to test it?

Run the new regression tests

`yarn test:api --testPathPattern="bidirectional-relations"`

Verify no regression in existing relation tests

`yarn test:api --testPathPattern="(bidirectional|unidirectional)-relations"`

## Manual Test

- Create a Tag content type (D&P) and a Product content type (D&P) with a bidirectional manyToMany to Tag
- Create and publish a Tag, create and publish a Product referencing that Tag
- Update the Tag's name and republish it
- `GET /api/products/:id?populate=tags&status=published`

Before fix: tags is empty or shows old data. 
After fix: tags contains the Tag with the updated name

## Related issue(s)/PR(s)

Fixes CMS-237
#23618
author: butcherZ

title: fix(content-type-builder): default draftAndPublish to true in AI CTB
body:
## What does it do?

Changes the default value of `draftAndPublish` from `false` to `true` when the AI Content-Type Builder creates a content type without explicitly setting the option. This aligns AI-created content types with the normal CTB behavior (`FormModal.tsx:214`).

## Why is it needed?

The AI CTB never enables Draft & Publish on content types it creates (reported in CMS-369). The external AI service doesn't set `options.draftAndPublish` in its schema output, and the transform layer (`toCTB.ts`) defaulted to `false`. The normal CTB defaults to `true`, so this was an inconsistency.

## How to test it?

1. Open the Content-Type Builder with AI chat enabled (EE, non-production)
2. Ask the AI to generate a content type (e.g. "Generate a product schema")
3. Verify the created content type has Draft & Publish enabled by default
4. Verify that if the AI explicitly sets `draftAndPublish: false`, it is still respected

## Related issue(s)/PR(s)

fix CMS-369

title: fix(graphql): expose status and hasPublishedVersion on non–D&P root queries for nested relations
body:
### What does it do?

Restores `status` and `hasPublishedVersion` on GraphQL root queries for **all** collection and single-type content types (including non–draft & publish types such as Users & Permissions `User`).
Previously (#25292) these arguments were only added when the content type had `draftAndPublish: true`, which removed them from non–D&P roots and broke clients that relied on them.

### Why is it needed?

On non–D&P parents, these arguments do not select draft/published **versions of the parent**, but they are still **valid and necessary**: the content API association resolver inherits them from the root query (`rootQueryArgs`) to apply draft vs published (and `hasPublishedVersion`) filters when loading **nested relations** whose **target** is draft & publish (e.g. User → related Articles). Without root-level `status` / `hasPublishedVersion`, nested D&P relations could not be queried as draft from a non–D&P entry ([#25746](https://github.com/strapi/strapi/issues/25746)).
A code comment documents a possible future direction (per-relation args on D&P targets instead of root inheritance).

### How to test it?

1. Enable the GraphQL plugin and open the GraphQL playground (or any GraphQL client).
2. Use a **non–D&P** root query (e.g. `usersPermissionsUsers` or another collection without D&P) and confirm the schema exposes `status` and `hasPublishedVersion` on that field.
3. Add a relation from that type to a **D&P** collection; run a query that loads that relation with `status: draft` vs `status: published` on the **root** field and assert nested results match (draft rows only when `draft`, published when `published`).
4. Optionally repeat with `hasPublishedVersion` if your setup uses it on nested D&P data.
5. Smoke-test a **D&P** root query (e.g. articles): behavior should remain unchanged.

### Related issue(s)/PR(s)

- https://github.com/strapi/strapi/issues/25746
author: innerdvations

reviews.body: LGTM! thanks for the fix and the comments about future directions :D

commits.message: fix: keep dp fields on non-dp graphql schema

title: security: package upgrades to remove deprecated versions of boolean, tar, and glob
body:
### What does it do?

**Direct dependency version bumps**

| Package               | From   | To     | Notes                                                                                                                      |
| --------------------- | ------ | ------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `glob`                | 13.0.0 | 13.0.6 | Root + `@strapi/core`                                                                                                      |
| `plop`                | 4.0.1  | 4.0.5  | Root + `@strapi/generators`                                                                                                |
| `tar`                 | 7.5.10 | 7.5.11 | `@strapi/cli-cloud`, `create-strapi-app`, `@strapi/data-transfer`                                                          |
| `global-agent`        | 3.0.0  | 4.1.3  | `@strapi/core`                                                                                                             |
| `@types/global-agent` | 2.1.3  | 3.0.0  | `@strapi/core` (dev)                                                                                                       |
| `inquirer`            | 8.2.5  | 9.3.8  | Root, `@strapi/core`, `@strapi/strapi`, `@strapi/admin`, `@strapi/data-transfer`, `@strapi/cli-cloud`, `create-strapi-app` |
| `rimraf`              | 5.0.5  | 6.1.3  | Root, `@strapi/admin`, `@strapi/data-transfer`, `@strapi/upgrade` (dev)                                                    |
| `supertest`           | 6.3.3  | 7.2.2  | Root, `@strapi/core` (dev), `@strapi/api-tests`                                                                            |
| `file-type`           | 21.0.0 | 21.3.3 | `@strapi/upload`                                                                                                           |
| `jwk-to-pem`          | 2.0.5  | 2.0.7  | `@strapi/plugin-users-permissions` (security bump)                                                                         |
| `better-sqlite3`      | 12.6.2 | 12.8.0 | Examples + `tests/app-template`                                                                                            |
| `mysql2`              | 3.9.8  | 3.20.0 | Examples (+ pinned versions in `examples/complex` v4 setup script)                                                         |
| `pg`                  | 8.11.1 | 8.20.0 | Examples (+ complex v4 setup script)                                                                                       |
| `@strapi/sdk-plugin`  | ^5.2.0 | ^6.0.1 | `examples/plugins/todo-example` (dev)                                                                                      |
| `@types/jscodeshift`  | 0.12.0 | 17.3.0 | `@strapi/generators`, `@strapi/upgrade` (dev)                                                                              |

_(Also: `@types/inquirer` 8.2.5 → 9.0.9 added to `@strapi/strapi` devDependencies; `jscodeshift` remains 17.3.0. Root and `docs/` lockfiles refreshed with `yarn dedupe`.)_

- Root and docs: **`yarn dedupe`**

**Dependency layout**

- **`yalc`**: `dependencies` → `devDependencies` in `@strapi/strapi`.
- **`copyfiles`**: removed from `@strapi/core` and `@strapi/strapi` dependencies; kept only under **`devDependencies`** in `@strapi/generators` (build-time).
- **`tar`**: dropped as a direct **root** devDependency (still pulled where packages need it).
- **`@types/tar`**: removed from `@strapi/data-transfer` devDependencies (aligned with `tar` usage).

**Code / test changes to support bumps**

- **`inquirer` v9 (ESM)**: default import / dynamic `import('inquirer')` and `inquirer.prompt(...)` across CLI packages, admin CLI, Strapi CLI helpers, and **`scripts/front/merge-duplicated-translations`** (inquirer loaded inside async IIFE); Jest uses **`moduleNameMapper`** → **`packages/core/strapi/src/test/mocks/inquirer.ts`** for Strapi package unit tests.
- **`global-agent` v4**: `Strapi.ts` uses named import `bootstrap` instead of `globalAgent.bootstrap()`.
- **CLI tests**: new **`tests/cli/tests/create-strapi-app/`** scaffold tests; **`tests/scripts/run-tests.js`** allows **`testApps: 0`** for the CLI domain and fixes pool handling

No breaking changes are expected

### Why is it needed?

Reduces deprecation warnings seen on Strapi install (try running a `npx create-strapi-app@5.41.0` to see)

Dependency bumps and layout changes shrink noisy transitive trees where possible; **`jwk-to-pem`** addresses a security-relevant package.

### How to test it?

These are all dependency upgrades; nothing should change. The most likely things to break would be the CLI tools, since `inquirer` has moved to esm only and required code changes.

### Related issue(s)/PR(s)

—

### Remaining work (follow-up PRs)

These are **not** covered by this PR but should clear all the rest of the install-time deprecation warnings:

- **ESLint** — upgrade (helps drop old **flat-cache** / **rimraf@3** chains from the toolchain).
- **Sendmail → Nodemailer** — replace the **sendmail** email provider with a **nodemailer**-based implementation (e.g. nodemailer’s sendmail transport) so **mailcomposer** / **buildmail** warnings go away.
- **Koa / `@koa/router`** — bump to current majors (**v15+** is a potential **breaking change** for plugin authors and apps, but may be necessary).
- **React Query** — move to **`@tanstack/react-query`** (addresses **rimraf@3** via **broadcast-channel** from v3).

title: fix(core): check devDependencies when resolving required admin dependencies
body:
### What does it do?

When checking if the peer deps are installed it now checks in devDeps as well

### Why is it needed?

Because those peerDeps are only needed to **build** the strapi admin, on the end user project they should be in devDeps and not on a production server, without this change strapi does not find the deps even if they are already installed

And so it will unnecessarily run multiple times the npm install command

```
[INFO] The Strapi admin needs to install the following dependencies:
strapi:build:    - react@^18.0.0
strapi:build:   - react-dom@^18.0.0
strapi:build:   - react-router-dom@^6.0.0
strapi:build:   - styled-components@^6.0.0
strapi:build: [INFO] Running 'npm install --legacy-peer-deps --save react@^18.0.0 react-dom@^18.0.0 react-router-dom@^6.0.0 styled-components@^6.0.0'
```

### How to test it?

An example package.json
```
{
  "name": "strapi",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "private": true,
  "description": "A Strapi application",
  "scripts": {
    "build": "strapi build",
    "deploy": "strapi deploy",
    "serve": "strapi develop",
    "seed:example": "node ./scripts/seed.js",
    "strapi": "strapi",
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@strapi/core": "^5.3.0",
    "@strapi/plugin-users-permissions": "5.3.0",
    "fs-extra": "^11.2.0",
    "mime-types": "^2.1.35",
    "mysql2": "3.11.4"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@strapi/pack-up": "^5.0.2",
    "@strapi/strapi": "5.3.0",
    "@types/node": "^22",
    "@types/react": "^18",
    "@types/react-dom": "^18",
    "react": "^18.3.1",
    "react-dom": "^18.3.1",
    "react-router-dom": "^6.28.0",
    "styled-components": "^6.1.13",
    "typescript": "^5"
  },
  "engines": {
    "node": ">=18.0.0 <=20.x.x",
    "npm": ">=6.0.0"
  },
  "strapi": {
    "uuid": "30c1208e-7af8-4b35-a99c-6c13a7c02d8e"
  }
}
```

This is only a first step because right now the strapi core includes the build command and so lists a [lot of build deps](https://github.com/strapi/strapi/blob/b0db56479de441dfe8feb37a43c7f6f6fecf75c1/packages/core/strapi/package.json#L132) as it's dependencies

Ideally the build part should be moved to a subpackage and become an optional peerDep of strapi core so that we don't need to have all those build tools like typescript, vite and all that on a production deployment
author: Tofandel

commits.message: Fix: lookup dependencies in devDeps as well
chore: use chaining to fix build error

title: enhancement: add customField parameter to extendFields
body:
enhancement: add customField parameter to args in app.getPlugin('content-type-builder').apis.forms.extendFields(..., {form: {advanced(args) => {...}}}) ### What does it do? Adds customField argument that can be used in plugin bootstrap in `app.getPlugin('content-type-builder').apis.forms.extendFields(..., {form: {advanced(args) => {...}}})`. Example with slightly modified i18n/admin/src/index.ts :
Example with slightly modified i18n/admin/src/index.ts
### Why is it needed? The solution allows plugin developers to have more flexibility when designing custom fields. When writing code in `advanced(args) => {...}`, at the moment there is no way to know whether current field is custom or built-in, even name of the field is unknown. Using customField argument along with contentTypeSchema (already there) allows to know what the current field exactly is.\ It is useful in case if there are few custom 'json' field types created and when creating content type, each field needs it's own configuration.
author: giollord

reviews.body: LGTM!

title: fix: Content types with attributes named filters, sort, fields, or populate no longer cause 400 validation errors on populate queries and countDraftRelations (#21338)
body:
<!--
Hello 👋 Thank you for submitting a pull request.

To help us merge your PR, make sure to follow the instructions below:

- Create or update the tests
- Create or update the documentation at https://github.com/strapi/documentation
- Refer to the issue you are closing in the PR description: Fix #issue
- Specify if the PR is ready to be merged or work in progress (by opening a draft PR)

Please ensure you read the Contributing Guide: https://github.com/strapi/strapi/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md
-->

### What does it do?

  Makes `convertNestedPopulate` in `packages/core/utils/src/convert-query-params.ts` schema-aware. Before treating a key as a query keyword (filters, sort, fields, populate, etc.), it now checks if the target schema has an attribute with that name. If it does, the key is skipped as a keyword, preventing the collision.

This matches the existing pattern in the traverse system (packages/core/utils/src/traverse/query-populate.ts, lines 21-25) which already uses schema-aware keyword detection.

  Change: Replace the blind destructure of query keywords with an `isKeyword` guard:
  
```
const isKeyword = (key: string) => !schema?.attributes?.[key];
  const filters = isKeyword('filters') ? subPopulateParams.filters : undefined;
  // ... same for sort, fields, populate, count, ordering, page, pageSize, start, limit
```
When schema is undefined (no target resolved), `isKeyword` returns true for all keys, preserving existing behaviour.

### !! IMPORTANT !! Edge case trade-off: 

**When a target schema has an attribute named filters (or any other keyword), users cannot use that keyword to filter/sort/select on that specific populate level. This is an inherent ambiguity — the same key can't be both an attribute and a keyword. Since users with keyword-named attributes currently get 400 errors on those populate paths, this is strictly an improvement.**

Changelog:
  - Fixed: Content types with attributes named filters, sort, fields, or populate no longer cause 400 validation errors on populate queries and countDraftRelations (#21338)

### Why is it needed?

When a content type has an attribute named filters (or sort, fields, populate), Strapi's query parameter parser treats the attribute name as a query keyword instead of a schema attribute. This causes 400 validation errors like:."Invalid key publishedAt at searchResultsPage.filters"

This affects the admin panel (countDraftRelations crashes when opening entries) and REST API populate queries. The issue has been reported since Strapi 5.0.0 and confirmed by multiple users.

### How to test it?

  1. Create a content type VDP with a string field named filters
  2. Create another content type Page with a relation searchResultsPage pointing to VDP
  3. Create a VDP entry and a Page entry related to it
  4. Open the Page entry in the admin panel
  5. Before fix: countDraftRelations returns 400 with "Invalid key publishedAt at searchResultsPage.filters"
  6. After fix: No error, entry loads normally

  Unit tests:
` npx jest packages/core/utils/src/__tests__/convert-query-params.test.ts --no-coverage`
 
test cases covering filters, sort, page collision, non-colliding keyword behavior, and dynamic zone fragments.

### Related issue(s)/PR(s)

Fixes CMS-148
#21338
author: butcherZ

commits.message: fix(utils): make convertNestedPopulate schema-aware to prevent keyword collision
test(content-manager): add API test for keyword attribute name collision (#21338)
fix(utils): prevent traverse recursion into keyword-named attributes in nested populate

getDeepPopulateDraftCount was applying a publishedAt filter to all
relation targets, including content types that don't have draft and
publish enabled. Since these types don't have a publishedAt attribute,
the query would crash with a 400 error.

The fix checks whether the target content type has draftAndPublish
enabled before adding the publishedAt filter. Non-D&P types are
skipped since they can't have drafts.

Fixes #21338
fix(content-manager): add safety guard for undefined targetModel
fix(content-manager): add hasDraftAndPublish guard to sumDraftCounts and getDeepPopulateDraftCount

title: chore(content-manager): optimize relations handling in EditView component
body:
### What does it do?

 Fixes the `selectFromResult` callback in `useGetRelationsQuery` to stop creating a new `data` object on every invocation. 
 The old code spread `data: { ...result.data, results: ... }`, which produced a new object reference every time — Now `results` and `pagination` are returned as flat top-level keys.

 Also wraps handlers and components in `useCallback` / `React.memo` to prevent excessive re-renders.

 ### Why is it needed?

 When editing an entity with multiple relation fields, scroll becomes laggy/jittery until all relation data loads.
 
 ### How to test it?

 1. `cd examples/getstarted && yarn develop`
 2. Open an entity with multiple relation fields in a modal
 3. Scroll while relations are loading — should be smooth, no jitter
 4. Connect/disconnect a relation — only the affected field should re-render
 5. Run tests: `npx nx run @strapi/content-manager:"test:front"`

 ### Related issue(s)/PR(s)

 Fix CMS-309

reviews.body: LGTM! should be good !

title: fix(content-manager): reduce excessive rerendering in components and dynamic zones
body:
### What does it do?

This PR reduces unnecessary re-renders for component fields and dynamic zones in the content-manager edit view.

Technical changes:
- memoizes the heavy component and dynamic zone subtrees so sibling items and nested fields can bail out when unrelated updates happen
- extracts field rendering inside components and dynamic zones into smaller memoized subcomponents
- narrows form subscriptions so component and dynamic zone wrappers subscribe only to the specific values they need instead of broader parent objects
- memoizes `FormLayout` and the `useDocument()` return object to reduce avoidable rerenders flowing through the edit view tree

### Why is it needed?

Before this change, component fields and dynamic zones were doing too much rendering

Symptoms included:
- editing any field in the contentType cause dynamic zones and components to re-render
- editing one component item could also cause other component items to re-render
- dynamic zone items showed similar unnecessary rerendering behavior
- on larger edit views, especially on lower-powered machines, this could contribute to visible input lag

### How to test it?

Environment:
- run the admin locally
- use a content type with:
  - a repeatable component
  - a non-repeatable component
  - a dynamic zone with multiple components/items
- ideally use a setup with several fields inside each component to make rerenders easier to observe

Path:
- Content Manager -> open an edit view for an entry using components and dynamic zones

Checks:
- confirm components or dynamic zones are not re-rendering because of changes in other components or in other fields
- check that behaviors are not changing or being affected by this change


### Related issue(s)/PR(s)
author: Bassel17

reviews.body:
The code looks good to me!
I'm still seeing pretty important input lags on DZ and components though, you can see here that the FPS drops down to around 50 and I still notice a serious input lag when typing:
https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f4a4091c-5f3e-4294-910d-4533bff0daea
I think this PR is still valuable, as it accomplishes the goals stated in your description (ie: confirm components or dynamic zones are not re-rendering because of changes in other components or in other fields), but this might not be sufficient to fix the input lag issues, so we'll need to continue to investigate that, wdyt?
This is working really well now, the FPS drops the tiniest bit (5 FPS maximum) but the input lag is gone, well done Bassel ✨👏🏻

commits.message: fix(content-manager): reduce excessive rerendering in components and dynamic zones
fix(content-manager): address review follow-ups in component/DZ rerendering
fix(content-manager): reduce nested component and dynamic zone rerenders

title: chore(core): parallelize and cache dynamic zone populate
body:
## What does it do?

Parallelizes dynamic zone population queries in the database layer to improve performance when populating content types with many DZ component types.

**Two changes:**

1. **Parallelized `morphToMany` per-type queries** (`packages/core/database/src/query/helpers/populate/apply.ts`) — When populating a dynamic zone, Strapi queries each component type sequentially in a `for` loop. With 10 component types, that's 10 sequential DB round-trips. This change runs them concurrently via `Promise.all`.

2. **Parallelized `applyPopulate` relation handlers** (same file) — The top-level orchestrator that processes all populate attributes (relations, media, morphToMany) also ran sequentially. Now runs concurrently.

3. **Memoized `getDeepPopulate`** (`packages/core/core/src/services/document-service/utils/populate.ts`) — Caches the populate config object since schemas don't change at runtime.

## Why is it needed?

Users with many component types in dynamic zones report slow population performance. The current workaround is a double lookup — first `populate: "*"` to discover which component types are present, then a targeted populate with only those types. This fix reduces the need for that workaround by making the single-pass populate significantly faster.

## Benchmark results

Tested against Strapi Cloud (remote Postgres) with a complex page-builder schema: 50 entries, 10 DZ section types with 3 levels of nesting (nested components, relations, media).

| Scenario | Before (P50) | After (P50) | Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full deep populate (all 10 sections) | 158.40 ms | 125.69 ms | **-20.6%** |
| Wildcard populate (`*`) | 202.15 ms | 165.32 ms | **-18.2%** |
| No populate (baseline) | 74.22 ms | 69.88 ms | ~same |

## How to test

### Setup

Ping me on slack for the setup

1. Copy the `api/` and `components/` folders from the `dz-populate-reproduction` folder into your Strapi project's `src/` directory. This adds:
   - 3 simple content types: `author`, `category`, `tag`
   - 10 complex DZ section components with nested components, relations, and media (`sections.*`, `cards.*`, `shared.*`)
   - A `dz-benchmark` content type with a dynamic zone using all 10 section types
2. Copy the `benchmarks/` folder to your project root
3. Create a strapi cloud project and deploy: `yarn deploy`
4. Create a **full-access API token** in the admin panel (Settings → API Tokens)
5. Run the benchamark script

```bash
# First run seeds 50 entries automatically
STRAPI_URL=https://your-app.strapiapp.com API_TOKEN=<your-token> node benchmarks/dz-populate.js
```
6. Update the package.json to use this experimental: `0.0.0-experimental.5506031e822d7cf75a5fc7435ac7eeb9bc85ebcd` and repeat all the steps again
7. Now compare the numbers

The "Full deep populate" and "Wildcard populate" scenarios should show ~20% improvement. "No populate" should be unchanged (confirms the improvement is in the population layer).
author: markkaylor

title: chore(.github): add PR milestone check
body: ### What does it do? This PR updates the existing `Checks` GitHub workflow and the `check-pr-status` action to enforce that all Pull Requests targeting the `develop` branch must have a milestone assigned. The `Checks` workflow now triggers on `pull_request` events including `milestoned` and `demilestoned` to ensure the status is updated when a milestone is added or removed. ### Why is it needed? To ensure better project management and tracking by making it a CI requirement for all PRs targeting the `develop` branch to have a defined milestone. ### How to test it? 1. **Create a PR targeting `develop` without a milestone:** The `check-pr-status` check should fail. 2. **Create a PR targeting `develop` with a milestone:** The `check-pr-status` check should pass. 3. **Create a PR targeting `main` (or any non-`develop` branch) without a milestone:** The `check-pr-status` check should pass. 4. **For an existing PR targeting `develop`:** * Remove its milestone: The `check-pr-status` check should fail. * Add a milestone: The `check-pr-status` check should pass. ### Related issue(s)/PR(s) N/A ---

title: chore(core): explicit Vite aliases for react, react-dom, react-router-dom, and styled-components
body: ### What does it do? Adds explicit Vite aliases for `react`, `react-dom`, `react-router-dom`, and `styled-components` in `packages/core/strapi/src/node/vite/config.ts`. This aligns Vite's dependency resolution strategy with webpack's existing behavior. ### Why is it needed? This change addresses an issue where the Admin UI crashes due to `styled-components` receiving `undefined` when creating a styled component (e.g., `styled(Popover.Arrow)`). The root cause is a singleton resolution gap in Vite, which allows multiple instances of `styled-components` (and other critical libraries) to be bundled, leading to runtime errors. This fix ensures these libraries resolve to a single physical install, preventing such conflicts. Fixes #25571 ### How to test it? 1. Reproduce the issue described in #25571 (e.g., by having a plugin that might cause duplicate `styled-components` instances). 2. Apply this PR. 3. Build and run the Strapi admin panel. 4. Verify that the Admin UI loads without crashing and the Guided Tour component functions correctly. ### Related issue(s)/PR(s) Fixes #25571 ---

title: fix(content-manager): reduce excessive rerendering in relation fields
body:
### What does it do?

This PR reduces unnecessary re-renders in the content-manager relation fields.

### Why is it needed?

Before this change, relation fields could re-render on unrelated field updates.

That was particularly expensive because the relation field contains a combobox/modal flow

On larger edit views, this extra rendering work can contribute to visible input lag and poorer responsiveness.

This PR reduces that work by making the expensive relation list subtree more stable and allowing memoization to actually be effective.

### How to test it?

Environment:
- run the admin locally
- use a content type that contains at least one relation field
- ideally test on an entry with multiple relations already connected

Path:
- Content Manager -> open any collection type edit view with a relation field

Checks:
1. Open an entry that has relation fields.
2. Edit unrelated fields in the form.
3. Confirm the relation field no longer re-renders excessively on every unrelated change.
4. Add a relation through the relation combobox/modal and confirm behavior is unchanged, also the excessive re-rendering in the relations of the fly modal should be gone
5. Remove a relation and confirm behavior is unchanged.
6. If possible, use React Scan or local render logging to compare rerender frequency before/after while typing in another field.

### Related issue(s)/PR(s)

title: fix(content-manager): reduce rerenders for conditional fields
body:
### What does it do?

This PR reduces unnecessary re-renders for conditional fields in the content-manager.

Technical changes:
- updates `InputRenderer.tsx` so conditional fields subscribe only to the form values used by their visibility condition instead of subscribing to the full form state
- applies the same conditional visibility optimization to`VersionContent.tsx`
- extracts the shared conditional dependency parsing/subscription helpers into `utils/conditionalFields.ts`

The goal is to keep conditional fields reactive to the fields they depend on, while avoiding rerenders when unrelated fields change.

### Why is it needed?

Before this change, conditional fields were effectively tied to broad form state updates, so changing any field could cause unrelated conditional fields to re-render.

On complex edit views, especially with many conditional fields, this creates avoidable rendering work and can contribute to visible input lag.

This PR narrows those subscriptions so conditional fields only re-render when:
- their own visibility dependencies change
- or we cannot safely infer dependencies and need to fall back to the broader subscription for correctness

### How to test it?

Environment:
- run the admin locally
- use a content type with conditional fields in the edit view
- ideally use a setup with multiple conditional fields depending on different source fields

Edit view checks:
1. Open an entry in the content-manager edit view.
2. Change a field that is **not** referenced by a given conditional field.
3. Confirm unrelated conditional fields do not re-render unnecessarily and the input remains responsive.
4. Change a field that **is** referenced by a conditional visibility rule.
5. Confirm the dependent conditional field updates visibility correctly.

History view checks:
1. Open the content history/version view for an entry with conditional fields.
2. Confirm fields with visibility conditions still render correctly.

Fixes CMS-383
author: Bassel17

reviews.body: Nice looks good, two small asks 🙏 ...about to test

commits.message: fix(content-manager): reduce rerenders for conditional fields

title: fix(openapi): correctly merge plugin router prefix with route paths
body:
### What does it do?

Fixes an issue in the OpenAPI generator where plugin route prefixes were not merged with their route paths.

`PluginRoutesProvider` was returning `router.routes` directly and ignoring `router.prefix`, causing prefixed plugin routes (e.g. upload) to be generated under `/` instead of their actual base path.

This change merges `router.prefix` with `route.path` and normalizes the resulting path to prevent duplicate or trailing slashes.

---

### Why is it needed?

When running: strapi openapi generate

Upload plugin routes were generated like:

```json
"/": {
  "operationId": "upload/post"
}
```
Instead of:
```json
"/upload": {
  "operationId": "upload/post"
}
```
This resulted in incorrect OpenAPI specifications for plugin routes.

### How to test it?

1. Checkout `develop`
2. Run yarn `build`
3. Navigate to `examples/kitchensink-ts`
4. Run `yarn develop`
5. Run `yarn strapi openapi generate`
6. Open `specification.json`

Before fix:
```json
"/": { "operationId": "upload/post" }
```
After fix:
```json
"/upload": { "operationId": "upload/post/upload" }
"/upload/files"
"/upload/files/:id"
```

### Related issue(s)/PR(s)

Fixes #25591
author: Akash504-ai

commits.message: fix(openapi): correctly merge plugin router prefix with route paths
fix(openapi): respect route-level prefix and use Core.Router type
test(openapi): add unit tests for plugin route prefix handling

title: fix(content-manager): reduce over-populated localizations and metadata
body: ### What does it do? Reduces the amount of data populated for the localizations relation and meta.availableLocales in Content Manager responses. - Adds withPopulateOverrids to the populate builder. Only populates minimal localization fields for content manager. - Updates the bulk locale publish dialog to only validate the current document locale client-side, since other locales no longer carry full data. Server-side validation still applies during the actual publish. ### Why is it needed? When a content type uses i18n with dynamic zones/components and has many locales, the CM endpoints deeply populate every localization and include heavy availableLocales metadata. This causes multi-MB payloads and hundreds of extra DB queries, making the admin panel very slow. On develop This branch ### How to test it? I recommend you first reproduce the issue on develop and then come to this branch to see the fix 1. Enable the i18n plugin and add 10+ locales. 2. Create a localized collection type with dynamic zones or components. 3. Create several documents and fill in translations for each locale. 4. Open the Content Manager list view and single entry view. 5. Inspect the network responses — payloads should be significantly smaller (localizations should only contain locale, documentId, publishedAt, updatedAt instead of full nested content). 6. title: Feat: persistent list view settings
body:
<!--
Hello 👋 Thank you for submitting a pull request.

To help us merge your PR, make sure to follow the instructions below:

- Create or update the tests
- Create or update the documentation at https://github.com/strapi/documentation
- Refer to the issue you are closing in the PR description: Fix #issue
- Specify if the PR is ready to be merged or work in progress (by opening a draft PR)

Please ensure you read the Contributing Guide: https://github.com/strapi/strapi/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md
-->

### What does it do?

It saves the display headers for the list view table and the filters, sort and pageSize in the local storage.


### Why is it needed?

This feature is needed to save time for the editor whenever they move either from one collection type to another one or edit anything else, the list view display headers, filters, sorting and pageSize are loaded again from before. This saves time for the editor as they don't need to always adjust their settings in the list view again. 

### How to test it?

Adjust the list view settings in the content manager listing page for one collection type. Then move to another page (can also be editing or creating an entry in that content type). And come back to that collection type listing page and the display headers, filters, sort and pageSize should be the same as before. This should not affect the other collection types. Make sure to also check that the other collection types are not affected from this change and that the persistence is only per collection type.

title: Chore: Content manager performance optimizations
body:
## What does it do?

This PR reduces unnecessary computation in Strapi v5's content-manager, improving overall performance.

**Core optimizations included:**

- **getMetadata Fast Path:**  
  For non-localized content types, an expensive `findMany` query (fetching all document versions for all locales/statuses) is replaced by a targeted `findOne` to retrieve only the opposite-status document. Skipping locale processing where it's unnecessary.

- **Populate Caching:**  
  Results from `getDeepPopulateDraftCount`, `getPopulateForValidation`, and `buildDeepPopulate` are now cached in module-scoped Maps. These previously recomputed on every request, leading to unnecessary overhead.

- **findPage Count Optimization:**  
  Strips out `populate` and `sort` parameters before calling `count()`, improving efficiency since `COUNT(*)` does not utilize these fields.

- **delete / publishMany Streamlining:**  
  Removes redundant calls to `buildDeepPopulate` during delete operations (when its result isn’t used). For `publishMany`, supplies an empty `populate` parameter as results do not require population.

- **List View Improvements:**  
  - Skips `getManyAvailableStatus` query entirely if Draft & Publish is disabled.
  - Uses `db.query.count` directly in place of the more expensive `findMany` for bulk action checks.
  - Avoids executing queries for empty localizations arrays.


## Why is it needed?

Strapi v5's content-manager has shown significantly slower performance than v4, particularly for deeply nested content types.

Despite prior optimizations (e.g., localizations populate validation), redundant queries, excessive population traversals, and repeated computation of schema helpers persisted. This PR directly targets and remedies these lingering inefficiencies.

**Performance Impact:**
Together with #25557, these changes were benchmarked against npm Strapi v5 (5.37.0) using complex schemas reported to be performing poorly 

```
| Endpoint            | Content Type | v4   | v5 stock | v5 patched | Faster than stock v5 | v5/v4 ratio (stock → patched) |
|---------------------|--------------|------|----------|------------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| List (pageSize=50)  | CT1          | 35ms | 78ms     | 61ms       | -22%                 | 2.2x → 1.6x                   |
| List (pageSize=50)  | CT2          | 31ms | 87ms     | 51ms       | -41%                 | 2.8x → 1.5x                   |
| List (pageSize=50)  | CT3          | 34ms | 85ms     | 57ms       | -33%                 | 2.5x → 1.7x                   |
| FindOne (edit view) | CT1          | 20ms | 59ms     | 48ms       | -19%                 | 3.0x → 2.7x                   |
| FindOne (edit view) | CT2          | 20ms | 48ms     | 38ms       | -21%                 | 2.4x → 1.7x                   |
| FindOne (edit view) | CT3          | 21ms | 66ms     | 54ms       | -18%                 | 3.1x → 2.8x                   |
```

- **List view**: Largest performance improvements (22-41% faster than npm latest v5). The v5/v4 overhead ratio drops from 2.2-2.8x to 1.5-1.7x—closing roughly half of the gap with v4.
- **Edit view**: Improves by ~20% due to the getMetadata fast path, which replaces a full-table scan with a single-row lookup. The v5/v4 ratio improves from 2.4-3.1x to 1.7-2.8x.

> Note: `countDraftRelations` showed negligible change. For the most complex content types, the v5/v4 ratio remains high. Further improvements to the document-service could be needed (parameter validation, transforms) and not covered by this PR.

## How to test

1. **Run unit tests**  
   ```bash
   cd packages/core/content-manager && yarn test:unit
   ```

2. **Manual verification**
   - Confirm the List view functions as expected.
   - Confirm the Edit view works correctly.
   - Draft relation counts are accurate for:
     - Non-localized types (both with Draft & Publish enabled and disabled).
     - Localized types (with Draft & Publish enabled).
   - Bulk publish/unpublish/delete actions succeed.


> **ℹ️  It is recommended to test this in combination with PR #25557** — both contribute to the overall performance improvements of the content manager.


fixes CMS-459

## What does it do?
  Exports the `ContentManagerPlugin` type from the content-manager package's main export, making it available for plugin developers.

## Why is it needed?
  Plugin developers who want to properly type their integrations with the content-manager (e.g., when using `app.getPlugin('content-manager')`) currently cannot import the `ContentManagerPlugin`
   type, forcing them to use `any` types or create custom interfaces.

## How to test it?
  1. Create a plugin that imports: `import type { ContentManagerPlugin } from '@strapi/content-manager/strapi-admin'`
  2. Verify TypeScript compilation succeeds

## Breaking changes?
  None - this only adds a type export that was already defined but not accessible.

### Related issue(s)/PR(s)

Fix #24148

title: fix(content-releases): publish in right order to preserve relations
body:
### What does it do?

In content releases, there's a bug when publishing two draft entries that have a relation. After publishing the release, we can't retrieve the relation when calling the API.
It's mainly because the two entries are published in parallel so the link between the two new published entries in the DB isn't created in the join table.

Video of the issue:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/519e8636-35cd-40f4-ae89-fb173dcfb9b5


After the fix:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/430c3771-8368-4da4-964d-717df669fbbe


### Why is it needed?

To fix an important issue with publishing relations through the content releases.

### How to test it?

- Create two collection types that are linked together with a relation (e.g. Article <-> Section) with Draft and Publish
- Create a draft of those collection types (don't forget to link them)
- Create a new release and put both your drafts in the release (to publish them)
- Publish the release
-> You should be able to retrieve the relation through an API call

### Related issue(s)/PR(s)

Resolves https://github.com/strapi/strapi/issues/24215

🚀
author: Adzouz

reviews.body:
I tested different scenarios and it works well for almost all of them ✨ 
Unfortunately, this fix doesn't work for relations inside components or dynamic zones, it publishes both but when fetching the main entry with the component/DZ with the other entry, it doesn't show in the API call:
https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d59c68d1-48a5-46b9-8b15-87411cf7625f
(I only tested one level deep, but I'm assuming deeply nested relations, for instance a component with a relation inside a dynamic zone) won't work either)
LGTM, thanks for the fix 🙏🏻✅
Just two small comments, I'll test it now
Tested and looks good nice work!

commits.message: fix(content-releases): publish entries in right order to preserve relations
fix: retrieve components and dynamic zones relations

title: chore(deps): bump jws from 3.2.2 to 3.2.3
body:
Bumps [jws](https://github.com/brianloveswords/node-jws) from 3.2.2 to 3.2.3.
Release notes

Sourced from jws's releases.

v3.2.3

Changed

  • Fix advisory GHSA-869p-cjfg-cm3x: createSign and createVerify now require that a non empty secret is provided (via opts.secret, opts.privateKey or opts.key) when using HMAC algorithms.
  • Upgrading JWA version to 1.4.2, addressing a compatibility issue for Node >= 25.
Changelog

Sourced from jws's changelog.

[3.2.3]

Changed

  • Fix advisory GHSA-869p-cjfg-cm3x: createSign and createVerify now require that a non empty secret is provided (via opts.secret, opts.privateKey or opts.key) when using HMAC algorithms.
  • Upgrading JWA version to 1.4.2, adressing a compatibility issue for Node >= 25.

[3.0.0]

Changed

  • BREAKING: jwt.verify now requires an algorithm parameter, and jws.createVerify requires an algorithm option. The "alg" field signature headers is ignored. This mitigates a critical security flaw in the library which would allow an attacker to generate signatures with arbitrary contents that would be accepted by jwt.verify. See https://auth0.com/blog/2015/03/31/critical-vulnerabilities-in-json-web-token-libraries/ for details.

2.0.0 - 2015-01-30

Changed

  • BREAKING: Default payload encoding changed from binary to utf8. utf8 is a is a more sensible default than binary because many payloads, as far as I can tell, will contain user-facing strings that could be in any language. (6b6de48)

  • Code reorganization, thanks @​fearphage! (7880050)

Added

  • Option in all relevant methods for encoding. For those few users that might be depending on a binary encoding of the messages, this is for them. (6b6de48)

title: fix(upload): add crossOrigin attribute to image preview
body: ### What does it do? Adds `crossOrigin="anonymous"` to the `` element used in the media library crop preview (`AssetPreview`). This prevents the browser from tainting the canvas when loading remote images. ### Why is it needed? When images are stored on an external provider (S3, R2, etc.), cropping fails with: `Failed to execute 'toBlob' on 'HTMLCanvasElement': Tainted canvases may not be exported.` ### How to test it? 1. Configure Strapi with any remote upload provider that has valid CORS. (Tested with `strapi-provider-cloudflare-r2`) 2. Upload an image, open Media Library → Crop. 3. Cropping should now work without errors. ### Related issue(s)/PR(s) Fixes #22745 Fixes #21074 Fixes #23742

title: feat: expand accordion by default when inserting a new component in a document
body:
### What does it do?
This PR updates the component insertion logic so that when a new component is added (in a dynamic zone), its accordion is automatically expanded by default.  
This ensures the newly inserted component is immediately visible without requiring the user to manually expand it.

### Why is it needed?

Currently, when adding a new component, it is inserted but its accordion remains collapsed.  
This makes it harder for users to notice the new component, especially in cases where multiple components are already present.  

By expanding the accordion automatically, the UX is improved as the new component is clearly highlighted and directly accessible.

### How to test it?

1. Run the application in development mode.  
2. Open the content editor and navigate to the Kitchen Sink collection type or another collection type that having dynamic zones.  
3. Add a new component.  
4. Verify that:
   - The new component is added to the list.  
   - Its accordion is automatically expanded.  
5. Add multiple components to confirm that each newly added component expands by default.  
6. The already existing components are correctly closed by default.

### Related issue(s)/PR(s)

No related issue found

### Note

This is my first PR in this project, I tried to implement this feature in the most compact and elegant way possible, in order to avoid breaking anything.  
I kindly ask reviewers for their benevolence during the review process.

The PR is ready to be merged.
author: Gouttfi

reviews.body: Hi @Gouttfi, thanks a lot for your contribution and sorry for the delay 🙏 I tested and it looks good to me 🙌

title: fix: single type publish permission error
body:
## What does it do?

Fixes the permission check in the single-type `publish` controller to allow users with only `publish` permission (without `update` or `create`) to publish existing drafts.

The controller now follows the same logic as `collection-types`:
- If the user has `update` permission on the document → updates then publishes
- If the user only has `publish` permission → publishes the existing draft without updating it
- If the document doesn't exist and user has `create` permission → creates then publishes

## Why is it needed?

Previously, users with `read` + `publish` permissions (but without `update` or `create`) would get a 403 Forbidden error when trying to publish a single type. This was caused by the `publish` method always calling `createOrUpdateDocument`, which requires either `update` or `create` permissions.

This bug prevented implementing proper editorial workflows where some users can only approve and publish content created by others, without being able to modify it.

## How to test it?

1. Create a role with only `read` and `publish` permissions for a single type (without `update` or `create`)
2. Create a user with that role
3. As an admin, create a draft for the single type
4. Log in as the restricted user
5. Navigate to the single type
6. Click the "Publish" button
7. Verify the document is published successfully without a 403 error

## Related issue(s)/PR(s)
Fix [#23603](https://github.com/strapi/strapi/issues/23603)

# Fix: Allow publishing single types with publish-only permission

## What does it do?

Fixes the permission check in the single-type `publish` controller to allow users with
only `publish` permission (without `update` or `create`) to publish existing drafts.

The controller now follows the same logic as `collection-types`:
- If the user has `update` permission on the document → updates then publishes
- If the user only has `publish` permission → publishes the existing draft without
  updating it
- If the document doesn't exist and user has `create` permission → creates then publishes

## Why is it needed?

Previously, users with `read` + `publish` permissions (but without `update` or `create`)
would get a 403 Forbidden error when trying to publish a single type. This was caused by
the `publish` method always calling `createOrUpdateDocument`, which requires either
`update` or `create` permissions.

This bug prevented implementing proper editorial workflows where some users can only
approve and publish content created by others, without being able to modify it.

## How to test it?

1. Create a role with only `read` and `publish` permissions for a single type (without
   `update` or `create`)
2. Create a user with that role
3. As an admin, create a draft for the single type
4. Log in as the restricted user
5. Navigate to the single type
6. Click the "Publish" button
7. Verify the document is published successfully without a 403 error

## Related issue(s)/PR(s)
Fix [#23603](https://github.com/strapi/strapi/issues/23603)
